message:
chore(beads): Close wa-nu4.1.1.4 (WaitCondition implementation)

WaitConditionExecutor implementation complete with:
- Pattern wait using PatternEngine and pane text polling
- PaneIdle wait using OSC 133 ShellState (deterministic)
- Heuristic fallback for shells without OSC 133 integration
- 52 workflow tests passing

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -121,7 +121,7 @@
 {"id":"wa-4vx.6.5","title":"Structured logging baseline: tracing spans, per-pane correlation ids, log-to-file option","description":"# Task: Structured logging baseline

## Goal
Make `wa` debuggable under real load and make E2E/diagnostic artifacts useful.

This is the baseline logging layer used by:
- `wa watch` runtime
- robot/human CLI commands
- E2E harness artifacts (`wa-4vx.10.6`, `wa-4vx.10.11`)

## Requirements
- Use `tracing` + `tracing-subscriber` (no ad-hoc `println!`).
- Support both:
  - human-friendly pretty logs (interactive default)
  - JSON logs (machine-parseable; required for CI/E2E/ops)

## Required fields / correlation
Key spans and events must include (where applicable):
- `workspace`
- `domain`
- `pane_id`
- `window_id`/`tab_id` (when available)
- `rule_id` / `event_id`
- `workflow_name` / `execution_id`
- `action_id` (audit linkage)

## Configuration
- `log_level`:
  - from config
  - overridable via `RUST_LOG`
- `log_format`: `pretty | json`
- `log_file`: optional path; when set, logs are written to file (E2E/diag bundles rely on this)

## Safety / redaction
- Never log raw pane contents.
- Any user-provided text that could contain secrets must be logged only via the redaction layer (`wa-4vx.8.3`).
- JSON logs must not include secrets, device codes, auth tokens, cookies, or full DOM dumps.

## Implementation notes
- Prefer a single global subscriber configured once at startup.
- Ensure JSON logging is valid even under concurrent spans.
- Include a stable timestamp field for JSON logs.

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - logger initialization does not panic
  - JSON log lines parse as JSON
  - required correlation fields appear on key spans (where practical via helper macros)
  - redaction invariants: known secret patterns do not appear in emitted log lines

- E2E:
  - harness captures logs reliably and includes them in artifacts (`wa-4vx.10.6`, `wa-4vx.10.11`)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Running `wa watch` emits structured logs with pane/domain correlation fields.
- JSON logging mode produces parseable JSON lines.
- E2E runs can enable verbose logging and capture it to an artifacts directory without code changes.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.6.6","title":"Watcher IPC receiver: accept user-var events over local socket; update state; emit signals","description":"# Task: Watcher IPC receiver (user-var events)

## Goal
Allow short-lived `wa event` processes (spawned by WezTerm) to deliver pane signals to the long-running watcher.

## Design
- Watcher opens a local unix socket (path in config).
- Message format: small JSON envelope:
  - pane_id
  - event_type (e.g., user_var, status_update)
  - name
  - value (raw + optional decoded JSON)
  - received_at
- Receiver task:
  - accepts connections
  - reads one message
  - validates size limits
  - enqueues into the event bus as a `Signal` event

## How the watcher uses the signal
- Update per-pane state if relevant (e.g., prompt boundary checkpoint or status_update metadata).
- Optionally persist as a separate table (`pane_signals`) if we want historical record.

## Failure behavior
- If message is invalid/too large: reject with an error (do not crash watcher).
- Logging: do not log raw payload content by default.

## Testing
- Unit/integration test starts the receiver, sends a synthetic event, and observes it on the event bus.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A synthetic `wa event` message reaches the watcher and appears on the internal event bus.
- Invalid payloads are rejected safely with clear errors and without logging raw secrets.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.6.7","title":"Watcher single-instance lock: prevent multiple wa watch writers per workspace","description":"# Task: Watcher single-instance lock (workspace guard)

## Goal
Prevent accidental multi-instance runs of `wa watch` that:
- compete for the same SQLite DB and cause busy loops or confusing interleavings
- duplicate ingest work and distort event timelines
- race on IPC endpoints (user-var lane socket)

Users should get a clear, actionable error instead of subtle corruption or "it feels flaky".

## Behavior (v0)
- On startup, `wa watch` acquires an exclusive lock scoped to the selected workspace/DB.
- Lock file lives under the workspace scope (`wa-4vx.9.6` conventions).
- If the lock is already held:
  - exit non-zero with a stable error code
  - print a message that includes:
    - workspace path
    - pid/host if recorded
    - a safe remediation (stop the other watcher, or run with an explicit override)

## Locking details
- Use an OS-level file lock (not just a pid file).
- Record diagnostic metadata adjacent to the lock (pid, started_at, wa version) for humans.

## Overrides (escape hatch)
Provide an explicit, scary flag (proposed): `--dangerous-disable-lock`.

Rules:
- If `--dangerous-disable-lock` is used, require the user to also provide an explicit `--db <path>` that is distinct, OR print a warning that they may corrupt timelines.

The goal is to make “I really want two watchers” possible, but never accidental.

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests:
  - first instance acquires lock successfully
  - second instance fails with stable error + hint
  - lock is released on clean shutdown
  - crash-y path: simulate a process exit and confirm the lock is not permanently wedged

## Acceptance Criteria
- Running two `wa watch` processes against the same workspace fails fast and explains why.
- The lock mechanism is robust across crashes (lock releases when process dies).

Build passes; tests blocked by unrelated lifetime errors in policy.rs.","compaction_level":0,"labels":["area-daemon","area-ux","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.6.7","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.6","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-18T10:29:35.106625597Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.6.7","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.6.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T10:29:58.515558775Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.6.7","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.9.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T10:52:09.546809924Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"wa-4vx.6.8","title":"Watcher lifecycle hardening: SIGINT/SIGTERM shutdown + panic crash report","description":"# Task: Watcher lifecycle hardening

## Goal
Make `wa watch` behave like a trustworthy daemon:
- shuts down cleanly on SIGINT/SIGTERM
- flushes and closes storage without leaving the DB in a confusing partial state
- produces actionable crash artifacts on panic

This is pure UX/reliability: users must be able to stop wa without fear.

## Requirements
### Graceful shutdown
- On SIGINT/SIGTERM:
  - stop ingest polling
  - drain/close channels
  - flush StorageHandle writer queue
  - close IPC endpoints
  - release workspace lock
- Emit a short shutdown summary (logs):
  - elapsed
  - final queue depths
  - last persisted seq per pane (optional if cheap)

### Panic/crash reporting
- Install a panic hook that:
  - writes a crash report artifact in the workspace artifacts dir
  - includes backtrace + build/version + workspace id
  - includes last-known health snapshot (queue depth, ingest lag)
  - is redacted (no raw pane text)

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - shutdown path drains queue and closes cleanly
- Integration tests:
  - synthetic ingest load + SIGINT simulation → clean exit
  - panic simulation → crash report file layout + redaction
- E2E:
  - `wa-4vx.10.15` graceful shutdown
  - `wa-4vx.10.21` `wa stop` coordination

## Acceptance Criteria
- Ctrl-C during heavy ingest does not lose already-enqueued writes.
- A panic produces an artifact that enables debugging without reproducing.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.7","title":"[EPIC] Robot mode CLI (stable JSON envelope + core commands)","description":"# Robot mode CLI

## Goal
Provide an agent-first CLI surface that is:
- stable (schema + error codes)
- token-efficient
- composable
- functionally complete for core operations

Robot mode is how an AI agent controls wa without relying on UI.

## Contract
Every robot command returns a consistent JSON envelope:
- ok/data/error/hint/elapsed_ms/version/now

## Core commands (minimum)
- `wa robot state` — list panes + inferred agent/state
- `wa robot get-text <pane_id>` — read tail or full text
- `wa robot send <pane_id> <text>` — send input (guarded)
- `wa robot wait-for <pane_id> <pattern>` — wait with timeout
- `wa robot search <fts query>` — query SQLite FTS
- `wa robot events` — recent/unhandled events
- `wa robot workflow <name> <pane_id>` — trigger workflow (Phase 2 will implement)
- `wa robot quick-start` — high-density help output

## Why this exists
Human-first CLIs are too verbose and inconsistent for agent consumption.
Robot mode must be deterministic so agents can compose reliable loops.

## Acceptance
- All commands work without interactive prompts.
- Error codes are stable and actionable.



## Success Criteria
- `wa robot` commands have stable JSON envelopes and versioned schemas for outputs/errors.
- Robot surfaces can query panes/state/events/search and perform policy-gated actions (send/workflow) safely.
- All robot outputs are validated in unit tests against JSON schemas.
- E2E scripts exercise robot commands as part of end-to-end scenarios with detailed artifacts.


## Testing
- Schema validation:
  - Robot outputs and errors are validated against versioned JSON schemas (see `wa-4vx.7.10`).
  - Tests must validate stable `error.code` values and stable envelope shape across commands.

- Contract tests:
  - For each command, include “happy path” + at least one failure mode (pane missing, invalid args, DB not initialized, policy denied).
  - Ensure error messages remain actionable but do not leak secrets.

- E2E coverage:
  - Robot commands are exercised as building blocks inside end-to-end scenarios (search, send gating, workflow triggers) with artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.7.1","title":"Robot mode scaffolding: CLI subcommands + stable JSON envelope + error codes","description":"# Task: Robot mode scaffolding

## Goal
Build the foundational CLI plumbing for `wa robot`.

Robot mode is an API surface for other tools/agents. It must be:
- stable
- machine-parseable
- explicit about errors and hints

## Requirements
### Subcommand structure
- Define a clear subcommand tree per plan.
- Centralize handler dispatch.

### Global flags (critical UX)
All robot subcommands must accept consistent global flags:
- --workspace <path> / WA_WORKSPACE
- --config <path> (optional)

Robots must not guess where the DB is.

### Output contract
- Stable JSON envelope:
  - ok / data / error / hint / elapsed_ms / version / now
- Stable error codes (string constants).

## Deliverables
- CLI parsing + dispatch to handlers.
- Shared response builder.
- Shared "context" builder that resolves:
  - effective config
  - workspace paths (DB/lock/socket/log)
  - and hands them to handlers

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests:
  - envelope shape is stable and schema-validated (`wa-4vx.7.10`)
  - unknown subcommand yields a structured error
  - `--workspace` changes scope deterministically
  - handlers can be tested with a fixture workspace without a real WezTerm

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa robot help` prints stable JSON.
- Unknown subcommand yields a structured error.
- Passing --workspace changes the DB/workspace scope deterministically.
","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:00:04.224019257Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-19T07:19:10.288340060Z","compaction_level":0,"labels":["area-robot","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.7.1","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:00:05.334634584Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.7.1","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:00:04.225588002Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.7.1","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.9.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T11:01:08.918402049Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.7.1","depends_on_id":"wa-y2e","type":"relates-to","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:55:35.942537218Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"wa-4vx.7.10","title":"Robot JSON schemas: versioned envelope + per-command outputs, validated in tests","description":"# Task: Robot JSON schemas

## Goal
Make the wa robot API contract explicit, versioned, and mechanically validated.

Robot mode is an API. Treating it like "just CLI output" is how integrations break.

## Deliverables
- Versioned JSON Schema files committed to the repo, e.g.:
  - docs/json-schema/wa-robot-envelope.json
  - docs/json-schema/wa-robot-state.json
  - docs/json-schema/wa-robot-search.json
  - docs/json-schema/wa-robot-events.json
  - docs/json-schema/wa-robot-send.json
  - docs/json-schema/wa-robot-wait-for.json
  - docs/json-schema/wa-robot-accounts.json
  - docs/json-schema/wa-robot-accounts-refresh.json
  - docs/json-schema/wa-robot-workflow-run.json
  - docs/json-schema/wa-robot-rules-list.json
  - docs/json-schema/wa-robot-rules-test.json
  - **docs/json-schema/wa-robot-reservations.json**
  - **docs/json-schema/wa-robot-reserve.json**
  - **docs/json-schema/wa-robot-release.json**

- Clear versioning rules
  - bump schema versions intentionally
  - document backward/forward expectations (even if we don't promise long-term compatibility)

## Testing
- Robot mode tests validate outputs against schemas (not only snapshots).
- Schema validation runs in CI.
- Schema tests should also validate:
  - stable ordering where applicable
  - error objects include stable `code` values

## Acceptance Criteria
- A breaking output change requires updating schema + tests.
- Schemas are sufficient for another tool to generate types safely.
- All schema validations and robot output tests pass in CI.


LABELS: area-docs, area-robot, area-tests, phase-1

DEPENDS ON
  → ○ wa-4vx.7: [EPIC] Robot mode CLI (stable JSON envelope + core commands) ● P0
  → ○ wa-4vx.7.1: Robot mode scaffolding: CLI subcommands + stable JSON envelope + error codes ● P0

BLOCKS
  ← ○ wa-4vx.7.2: Implement `wa robot state` (live panes + DB enrichment + inferred agent/state) ● P0
  ← ○ wa-4vx.7.3: Implement `wa robot get-text` (tail/escapes options, robust errors) ● P0
  ← ○ wa-4vx.7.4: Implement `wa robot send` (PolicyEngine-gated, optional wait-for, timeout) ● P0
  ← ○ wa-4vx.7.5: Implement `wa robot search` (FTS query + scoping + snippet) ● P0
  ← ○ wa-4vx.7.6: Implement `wa robot events` (recent/unhandled, filter by pane/type) ● P0
  ← ○ wa-4vx.7.7: Quick-start mode (no args) + `wa robot quick-start` output tuned for agents ● P1
  ← ○ wa-4vx.7.9: [Robot] `wa robot wait-for` (pattern wait with timeout, robust errors) ● P1
  ← ○ wa-nu4.1.5.4: [Robot] `wa robot accounts` (list accounts + usage + pick preview) ● P1
  ← ○ wa-nu4.1.5.5: [Robot] `wa robot accounts refresh` (invoke caut refresh, update DB mirror) ● P1
  ← ○ wa-nu4.2.1.4: Pack tooling: `wa robot rules list/test`, pack linter, drift workflow (fixture-first) ● P1
  ← ○ wa-nu4.3.1.1: MCP API spec: tools/resources, schemas, error codes, versioning ● P1
  ← ○ wa-4vx.7.8: Robot mode tests: envelope stability, error codes, command outputs (synthetic) ● P2
  ← ○ wa-nu4.1.6.3: Robot/Human commands: reserve/release/list pane reservations ● P2
","notes":"Background / Why:\n- Robot mode is an API; schema stability prevents integration breakage.\n- Versioned schemas let us validate outputs and detect regressions early.\n\nConsiderations:\n- Treat schema_version as a compatibility contract.\n- Error envelopes must be fully specified and stable.\n\nLogging / Observability:\n- Emit structured schema validation failures with field paths and version.\n- Log schema_version per response at debug level.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T10:50:34.682634047Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T21:45:42.810905818Z","compaction_level":0,"labels":["area-docs","area-robot","area-tests","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.7.10","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-18T10:50:38.906301938Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.7.10","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.7.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T10:50:39.124710615Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.12","title":"wa robot workflow - JSON workflow trigger/status/abort","description":"## Summary
Implement `wa robot workflow` commands that allow AI agents to trigger, monitor, and abort workflows programmatically via JSON interface.

## Background & Rationale
From PLAN.md Appendix A.2 Robot Commands:
```
wa robot workflow <name> <pane_id> [--force]
```

And from MCP tools (A.3):
```
wa.workflow_run - Execute workflow
```

AI agents need programmatic workflow control to:
1. Trigger workflows manually when auto-handling is disabled
2. Monitor workflow execution status
3. Abort stuck or unwanted workflows
4. Chain workflows based on results

This is the robot-mode interface to the workflow engine (wa-nu4.1.1).

## Implementation Details

### Command Structure
```bash
# Start a workflow
wa robot workflow start <name> <pane_id> [--force]
wa robot workflow start handle_compaction 3

# Check workflow status
wa robot workflow status [--workflow-id <id>] [--pane-id <pane>]

# Abort a running workflow
wa robot workflow abort <workflow-id> [--force]

# List available workflows
wa robot workflow list
```

### JSON Output Schema - Start
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "command": "workflow.start",
  "data": {
    "workflow_id": "wf-abc123",
    "workflow_name": "handle_compaction",
    "pane_id": 3,
    "status": "started",
    "started_at": "2025-01-18T10:30:00Z"
  }
}
```

### JSON Output Schema - Status
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "command": "workflow.status",
  "data": {
    "workflow_id": "wf-abc123",
    "workflow_name": "handle_compaction",
    "pane_id": 3,
    "status": "running",
    "current_step": 2,
    "total_steps": 5,
    "step_name": "wait_for_prompt",
    "started_at": "2025-01-18T10:30:00Z",
    "elapsed_ms": 5234
  }
}
```

### Error Cases (from Appendix B concepts)
- E_WORKFLOW_NOT_FOUND: Workflow name doesn't exist
- E_WORKFLOW_LOCKED: Pane already has active workflow (unless --force)
- E_WORKFLOW_ABORTED: Workflow was aborted
- E_POLICY_DENIED: Policy engine denied workflow start
- E_PANE_NOT_FOUND: Target pane doesn't exist

### Implementation Notes
1. Integrate with workflow engine from wa-nu4.1.1.1
2. Respect per-pane workflow locks (wa-nu4.1.1.2)
3. Support --force to override locks (with audit)
4. Return workflow_id for subsequent status/abort calls
5. Include step-level progress for detailed monitoring

## Success Criteria
- [ ] `wa robot workflow start` triggers workflow and returns ID
- [ ] `wa robot workflow status` shows detailed progress
- [ ] `wa robot workflow abort` cleanly stops workflow
- [ ] `wa robot workflow list` shows available workflows
- [ ] --force overrides pane lock with audit
- [ ] Error codes are stable and actionable
- [ ] Unit tests for JSON serialization
- [ ] Integration test with mock workflow execution

## Dependencies
- wa-4vx.7.1 (robot scaffolding) - for JSON envelope and error codes
- wa-nu4.1.1.1 (workflow engine types) - for Workflow trait
- wa-nu4.1.1.2 (pane locks) - for lock management
- wa-nu4.1.1.5 (workflow runner) - for execution","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T15:29:10.811525254Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T15:33:35.385517965Z","closed_at":"2026-01-18T15:33:35.385517965Z","close_reason":"Duplicate/inapplicable: superseded by existing robot command beads (accounts: wa-nu4.1.5.4/wa-nu4.1.5.5; rules list/test: wa-nu4.2.1.4; robot workflow: wa-nu4.1.1.9).","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-nu4.1.1.12","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.7.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T15:29:16.882276647Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-nu4.1.1.12","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-18T15:29:10.812943005Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-nu4.1.1.12","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T15:29:17.128798500Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-nu4.1.1.12","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T15:29:17.381959699Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-nu4.1.1.12","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.1.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T15:29:17.634242814Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.2","title":"Per-pane workflow lock/reservation (prevent conflicting automations)","description":"# Task: Per-pane workflow lock/reservation

## Goal
Ensure only one workflow runs per pane at a time (or per configurable lock class).

## Why
Without locks, multi-step workflows can interleave and inject nonsense into a terminal. Locks are the first line of correctness for any automation system.

## Scope boundary
This is an **internal workflow lock**, not the user-visible pane reservation system (see the separate pane reservations epic). It prevents workflow collisions even when no external reservation is set.

## Design (v0)
- In-memory lock table keyed by `pane_id`.
- Lock acquisition/release is recorded in workflow execution logs.
- Optional future: persisted lock record for multi-process/distributed mode (explicit non-goal for v0).

## Failure behavior
- If a lock cannot be acquired:
  - either skip starting the workflow (preferred)
  - or mark the event as “deferred / in_progress elsewhere” depending on event semantics

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - two concurrent start attempts for the same pane: exactly one acquires lock
  - lock is released on workflow completion and on abort

## Acceptance Criteria
- If a workflow is running on pane X, new detections for X do not start another workflow.
- Locks are released on both success and failure paths.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.3","title":"Persist workflow_executions + workflow_step_log; resume after restart from last completed step","description":"# Task: Persist workflow runs + resume logic

## Goal
Make workflows durable so `wa watch` can restart without losing where it was.

## Requirements
- On workflow start, create `workflow_executions` row.
- On each step:
  - mark step started with an input snapshot (redacted)
  - on completion mark completed with output snapshot (redacted)
  - on failure mark failed with error detail and attempt count
- On daemon restart:
  - find running/incomplete workflows
  - reconstruct state and resume from last completed step

## Idempotency notes
- Resuming must not repeat already-completed steps.
- Step results should be written before side effects whenever possible.

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - resume computes next step index correctly given a step log
- Integration tests:
  - simulate “kill mid-workflow” and verify:
    - already-completed steps are not re-run
    - workflow completes after restart

## Acceptance Criteria
- Kill `wa watch` mid-workflow and restart: it resumes without repeating already-completed steps.
- Step logs are sufficient to understand what happened without consulting raw terminal text.

-{"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.4","title":"WaitCondition implementation: Pattern wait + PaneIdle via OSC 133 (fallback heuristics if needed)","description":"# Task: WaitCondition implementation

## Goal
Implement `wait_for_condition` correctly, efficiently, and in a way that is shared across robot/workflow code paths.

This is one of the core reliability primitives:
- it’s how workflows avoid spamming
- it’s how workflows become resumable and debuggable
- it’s how “verified sends” become possible

## PaneIdle
Prefer deterministic shell integration:
- `PaneIdle` == observe OSC 133 prompt marker.

Fallback (only if shell integration missing):
- heuristic prompt match (explicitly marked as best-effort)

## Pattern wait
- Poll get-text (tail window) using the shared PaneWaiter (`wa-4vx.2.6`).
- Stop early on success.
- Return timeout error on deadline.

## Implementation notes
- Do not re-implement polling loops inside the workflow layer; reuse PaneWaiter so:
  - workflow waits, robot wait-for, and send verification behave identically
  - backoff and cancellation semantics are consistent

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Pattern wait succeeds/fails deterministically on synthetic fixtures.
  - Timeouts are honored precisely (no “wait forever” bugs).
  - Cancellation produces a stable outcome and a debuggable step log entry.
  - PaneIdle transitions are correct for OSC 133 sequences.
  - Fallback heuristics are only used when deterministic signals are missing (and are clearly reported).

- Integration tests:
  - A synthetic workflow run that includes at least one wait step records stable step logs and does not spam sends.

## Acceptance Criteria
- WaitFor works reliably on synthetic fixtures.
- Timeouts/cancellations produce stable, debuggable step logs.

+{"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.4","title":"WaitCondition implementation: Pattern wait + PaneIdle via OSC 133 (fallback heuristics if needed)","description":"# Task: WaitCondition implementation

## Goal
Implement `wait_for_condition` correctly, efficiently, and in a way that is shared across robot/workflow code paths.

This is one of the core reliability primitives:
- it’s how workflows avoid spamming
- it’s how workflows become resumable and debuggable
- it’s how “verified sends” become possible

## PaneIdle
Prefer deterministic shell integration:
- `PaneIdle` == observe OSC 133 prompt marker.

Fallback (only if shell integration missing):
- heuristic prompt match (explicitly marked as best-effort)

## Pattern wait
- Poll get-text (tail window) using the shared PaneWaiter (`wa-4vx.2.6`).
- Stop early on success.
- Return timeout error on deadline.

## Implementation notes
- Do not re-implement polling loops inside the workflow layer; reuse PaneWaiter so:
  - workflow waits, robot wait-for, and send verification behave identically
  - backoff and cancellation semantics are consistent

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Pattern wait succeeds/fails deterministically on synthetic fixtures.
  - Timeouts are honored precisely (no “wait forever” bugs).
  - Cancellation produces a stable outcome and a debuggable step log entry.
  - PaneIdle transitions are correct for OSC 133 sequences.
  - Fallback heuristics are only used when deterministic signals are missing (and are clearly reported).

- Integration tests:
  - A synthetic workflow run that includes at least one wait step records stable step logs and does not spam sends.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.5","title":"Workflow runner: consume Detection events, select workflow, execute with policy gates","description":"# Task: Workflow runner/scheduler

## Goal
Bridge between detections/events and workflow executions.

## Responsibilities
- Subscribe to detection/event stream.
- Choose the highest-priority matching workflow.
- Enforce per-pane lock.
- Execute steps with:
  - policy checks before any input injection
  - bounded retries with backoff
  - step logging (durable)
  - clear “why did/didn’t we act?” outcomes

## Action execution architecture (important)
To avoid duplicated safety logic, the runner should not “roll its own” injection path. It should route any action attempt (send text, ctrl-c, etc) through a shared core helper that:
- calls PolicyEngine
- emits audit entries (when wired)
- performs the action only if allowed

This is how we ensure robot/human/workflow paths stay consistent.

## Event interaction
- The runner should cooperate with event semantics (`wa-nu4.1.1.10`):
  - set `in_progress` (if used)
  - mark handled on completion/abort/failure
  - avoid spamming repeat executions for the same event

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - workflow selection chooses correct workflow given a detection
  - lock prevents concurrent runs per pane
  - policy-denied action yields a stable denied outcome without calling send
- Integration tests:
  - synthetic event stream → creates workflow_executions + step logs

## Acceptance Criteria
- When a compaction detection arrives, `handle_compaction` workflow runs and records a completed execution.
- When policy denies an action, the workflow records an explainable denied/aborted result and does not inject input.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.6","title":"Data-driven workflow descriptors (YAML/TOML) for simple prompt injection sequences","description":"# Task: Data-driven workflow descriptors (YAML/TOML)

## Goal
Allow operator customization of **simple, safe workflows** without recompiling wa.

This is about *configuration-driven prompt sequences* ("wait for X, then send Y"), not complex logic.

## Scope (strict)
Only support a small, explicitly safe step vocabulary:
- `wait_for` (substring/regex) with timeout
- `sleep` (bounded)
- `send_text` (must go through PolicyEngine; can be denied)
- `send_ctrl` (limited set like Ctrl-C; also policy gated)

Non-goals:
- loops/conditionals
- arbitrary shell execution
- custom code hooks

## Descriptor model
- Versioned schema (e.g., `workflow_schema_version: 1`).
- Strong validation at load time:
  - unknown fields rejected
  - timeouts and sleep durations bounded
  - maximum number of steps bounded
  - step ids unique for audit/logging

## Execution integration
- Descriptors compile into the same internal step executor used by Rust workflows.
- Every send step uses the shared policy-gated injection helper.
- Every step writes a step log entry (same durability semantics as native workflows).

## Security & redaction
- Descriptor files may contain prompt text; treat them as potentially sensitive.
- Logs/audit should record only a redacted summary of sent text.

## Testing strategy
- Unit tests:
  - parsing + validation (good and bad fixtures)
  - schema version errors
  - bounding behavior (too many steps, too-long timeout) rejects

- Integration tests:
  - execute a sample descriptor against a fake pane/query environment
  - assert step logs emitted and policy gating is invoked

## Acceptance Criteria
- A sample YAML workflow can be loaded, validated, and executed.
- Invalid descriptors fail with precise error messages and remediation hints.
- Send steps are policy-gated and produce audit + step logs.
","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:03:52.174200175Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T13:05:54.207039504Z","compaction_level":0,"labels":["area-workflows","phase-2"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-nu4.1.1.6","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:03:52.202754107Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-nu4.1.1.6","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:04:01.783254803Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.7","title":"Workflow engine tests: lock behavior, step logging, restart resume, wait conditions","description":"# Task: Workflow engine tests

## Goal
Prevent regressions in the core automation engine.

## Testing
- Locking:
  - per-pane lock prevents concurrent workflows
  - lock release behavior on success/abort

- Durability:
  - step logs written correctly
  - resume from last completed step after restart

- Wait conditions:
  - success path
  - timeout path

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests cover concurrency, durability/resume, and wait behavior with deterministic fixtures.
- Failures are actionable (which step/state transition failed).
